title: Cuban black beans
subtitle: Frijoles negros slow-cooked with sofrito, cumin, and oregano.
excerpt: The Cuban kitchen staple, frijoles negros. Black turtle beans cooked slowly with a sofrito of onion, green pepper, and garlic, finished with cumin, oregano, and a splash of vinegar. Served over rice as the main meal or as a side to roast pork. Two hours mostly hands-off; serves six.
sourceNotes:
Cuban home cookery, documented widely in twentieth-century Caribbean cookbooks. Frijoles negros sit at the centre of Cuban daily eating, served over white rice as moros y cristianos when cooked together, or alongside rice as the standard plate.

The sofrito of onion, green pepper, and garlic, built in olive oil with cumin and dried oregano, is the flavour base. The beans absorb the sofrito as they simmer, taking on a sweet vegetable depth that thin-cooked beans lack.

A splash of vinegar or dry sherry at the end is canonical and lifts the dish.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes excellently for up to three months. The beans hold their shape and the sauce thickens further; defrost in the fridge overnight and reheat slowly with a splash of water if it has gelled.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly. A wide deep pan helps the beans cook evenly.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Better on day two. The flavours settle overnight; reheat gently with a splash of water and adjust the vinegar at the end.

recipeTools.notes: A heavy 4-litre saucepan or Dutch oven, with a tight lid.
Optional, for crushing a ladleful of beans to thicken the sauce.

glossaryTerms.definition: A slow-cooked base of finely chopped onion, garlic, and (in Cuban cookery) green pepper in olive oil, used as the flavour foundation for many Spanish, Caribbean, and Latin American dishes. The cooking is unhurried; the vegetables sweat and soften without browning.

content.text: Cuban black beans are the daily pot of the Cuban kitchen. Black turtle beans simmered slow with a soffritto of onion, green pepper, garlic, and dried oregano, scented with cumin, finished with a splash of vinegar that lifts the whole thing. Eaten over white rice as the main meal, or as a side to roast pork, fried plantain, and the rest of the Cuban supper repertoire.
The flavour base is the
sofrito

, a slow-cooked sweat of onion and green pepper in olive oil. The Cuban sofrito is distinct from the Caribbean Hispanic ones with tomato and herbs; this one is leaner and lets the bean carry the dish.

Two hours on the hob, mostly unattended. The beans soak overnight, simmer slowly, then sit for thirty minutes off the heat to thicken. Tinned beans cut this in half but the texture and flavour drop noticeably; if you have time, soak from dry.
What you need

items.prepNote: dried, soaked overnight in plenty of cold water

content.text: Method
Cook the beans first

Drain {{black-beans}} of soaked beans. Tip into a large saucepan; cover with fresh cold water to 5 cm above the beans. Add {{bay-leaves}} of bay. Bring to a simmer, skim any foam, then reduce to a bare bubble and cook with the lid ajar for an hour. The beans should be tender but still holding shape. Drain and keep the cooking liquid back.
Build the sofrito

In the same pan, set {{olive-oil}} of olive oil over a medium heat. Add the finely chopped {{onion}} of onion and {{pepper-green}} of green pepper. Cook for ten minutes, stirring often, until soft and translucent. Do not brown. Stir in {{garlic}} of garlic, {{cumin-ground}} of cumin, and {{oregano-dried}} of dried oregano. Cook for two more minutes until fragrant; the oil should be visibly tinted from the spices.
Combine and simmer

Return the drained beans to the sofrito pan. Pour in 800 ml of the bean cooking liquid (top up with water if you have less). Stir through and bring to a low simmer. Cook uncovered for thirty to forty minutes, stirring occasionally. The liquid reduces and thickens; the beans absorb the sofrito flavour.
Crush some beans to thicken

Lift a ladleful of beans out and crush them roughly with a fork or masher. Stir back into the pan. The crushed beans thicken the sauce naturally; the finished texture should be loose, glossy, and shine. Cook five more minutes.
Finish with sherry and vinegar

Stir in {{sherry-dry}} of dry sherry if using and {{red-wine-vinegar}} of red wine vinegar. Add {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and black pepper. Taste and adjust. The vinegar at the end is canonical; it lifts the heaviness of the bean and balances the sweetness of the sofrito. Pull off the heat and rest, covered, for ten minutes before serving.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: Black beans turn on the soak and the sofrito. The two failure modes are tough beans and a thin sauce.

items.symptom: Beans firm and chalky after two hours.
items.cause: Beans old or soaked too briefly.
items.fix: Dried beans older than a year never soften properly. Buy fresh stock; soak for at least twelve hours. If the beans still resist after an hour of simmering, give them another twenty minutes before adding to the sofrito.

items.cause: Too much cooking liquid or simmer too short.
items.fix: Continue simmering uncovered for another fifteen to twenty minutes to reduce. Crush more of the beans with a fork or masher to thicken naturally.

items.cause: Heat too high or pan too hot when the garlic went in.
items.fix: The sofrito wants a steady medium heat; the vegetables sweat rather than fry. Add the garlic only when the onion and pepper are soft and translucent, and keep the heat low so the garlic does not catch.

items.cause: Salt added too early or skipped vinegar.
items.fix: Salt and vinegar both go in at the end. Bean dishes need salting once the beans are cooked through; salt in the soaking water keeps them firm but does not season them. The vinegar lifts everything.

content.text: Variations

Some Cuban cooks add half a teaspoon of brown sugar at the end, which rounds the vinegar. Others add a slice of bacon or a small piece of ham bone to the simmer, turning the dish meaty. The vegetarian base is what holds up across the Caribbean diaspora; both add-ins are regional touches.

Moros y cristianos is the rice-and-beans version, in which the rice cooks in the bean liquid and the two dishes combine in one pot. The Puerto Rican habichuelas guisadas uses red beans and tomato instead and is a different dish in spirit.
Make ahead, freezing, leftovers
Cuban black beans get better on day two. Cool, refrigerate, and reheat gently with a splash of water if the sauce has gelled. Three to four days in the fridge is the safe window.

Freezes excellently for up to three months. Portion into containers, leaving room for expansion, and freeze. Defrost in the fridge overnight; reheat slowly with a splash of water and refresh the vinegar at the end if the flavour reads flat.
Where this dish lives

Frijoles negros are the daily bean of Cuban kitchens, eaten with rice at lunch and dinner across the island and across the Cuban diaspora in Miami, New York, and beyond. The black turtle bean carries the Caribbean Hispanic table the way kidney beans carry Jamaican rice and peas and pinto beans carry Mexican rice.

The dish has Spanish roots in the bean and pulse stews of Andalusia and Asturias, brought across in the colonial period and married to the Caribbean black bean which was already a New World staple. The result is one of the cleanest examples of Old-meets-New World cookery on the modern table.
